text.title: K-Factor Decomposition and Optimization Priority
text.summary: K = i * c formula, cycle time as secondary lever, funnel metric definitions, and optimization priority rule (c before i).

rule.statement: Decompose K into i (avg invites sent per user) and c (invitee conversion rate = signups / invites sent) before deciding where to optimize; optimize c first.
rule.rationale: Improving c lifts every future loop turn because all future invites benefit from higher conversion; improving i only increases volume into the current conversion rate.

list.item: i = total invite_sent events / distinct active users in period
c = invitee_signup events matched to invite tokens / total invite_sent events
cycle_time = median days between invite_sent and invitee_signup

rule.statement: Measure cycle time (p50 days from invite_sent to invitee_signup) and treat it as a primary optimization lever alongside i and c.
rule.rationale: K = 0.4 with 3-day cycle time beats K = 0.7 with 60-day cycle time over a quarter in compounding terms.

list.item: reduce cycle time by: one-click mobile signup, pre-filled email, social login for invitees
track cycle_time weekly; a sudden increase signals friction introduced in a signup change
invite_shown: % of active users who see the invite prompt
invite_sent: % of users who actually send an invite (= share rate)
invite_clicked: clicks on the invite link by recipients
invitee_active: invitees who reach the activation milestone within D7

rule.statement: Track invitee D30 retention separately from organic D30; if invitee retention is <50% of organic, the loop is poisoning the cohort.
rule.rationale: Incentivized loops attract users who join for the reward, not the product; high K with low invitee retention grows the user count but collapses LTV.
